data.title: Worried About Inflation? Old Republic Offers Protection Through Growth And Dividends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Old Republic International Corporation (ORI) has been operating for over a century with a strong business model that has provided market-beating returns for decades. Their unique position within niche commercial insurance offerings results in strong retention rates and creates a "sticky" product which is rare in an industry often characterized by low customer loyalty. The price today represents a fair value for a great business. Investors may purchase today and start collecting solid dividends or wait for a pullback to improve future total returns.

Business Model

Old Republic has two main segments – Specialty and Title. The company places more importance on the former so I’ll start with that one.

Specialty Insurance

ORI’s specialty insurance operation writes commercial lines for auto (42% of 2024 net premiums earned), workers comp (18%), inland marine and CMP (13%), general liability (8%), and financial indemnity (7%) along with personal home and auto warranties (7%). 96% of these policies are marketed through independent insurance agencies and brokers, meaning ORI has little direct contact with customers. There’s little customer loyalty with any type of insurance, but Old Republic’s indirect sales result in a brand cache that is essentially null. And yet, the company’s retention rate is over 90%. How do they pull that off?

The answer lies in how ORI structures its policies. Much of the company’s business is written through captive insurance programs, retrospectively rated policies and large deductible plans. Commercial auto made up 42% of specialty insurance premiums earned in 2024 through subsidiaries ORINSCO and Great West, so I’ll use that business as an example.

Drive down any American highway and it won’t take long to spot an attorney-sponsored billboard gloating about the millions they’ve earned from claims related to semi-truck accidents. With thousands of trucks on the road, trucking companies require substantial insurance coverage to limit financial risk associated with the accidents which are bound to occur. Rather than calling Allstate (ALL) and obtaining policies for each vehicle and driver, the company might set up a “captive insurer”. This entity, a directly owned subsidiary, effectively insures the parent company’s liability risk. Because the trucking company doesn’t want the distraction of running an insurance business (and because completely self-insuring doesn’t actually limit any financial risk) they’ll bring in a company like Great West or ORINSCO (ORI subsidiaries) to “front” the operation. These fronting companies charge a fee to handle administrative tasks such as evaluating and paying claims. They also charge insurance premiums for claims to the captive beyond a specified deductible amount. Because most companies that set up captive insurance are relatively large, these policies typically have a large deductible. This allows the trucking company in our example to keep down insurance premiums while still insuring against catastrophic losses. The fronting insurance partner (like ORI) would then pay all claims and bill deductible amounts back to the captive’s parent company (the trucking company in this case).

ORI also writes specialty insurance policies under large deductible plans and retrospectively rated plans. Large deductible insurance is fairly self-explanatory. Retrospectively rated plans are characterized by premiums which adjust after the fact to account for actual claims submitted during the policy period. These policies, along with captive insurance, limit risk for Old Republic and encourage insured companies to take steps to prevent accidents. ORI charges premiums based on their own claims history as well as that of the insured company. A company with a stellar safety record will pay considerably lower premiums than one that has a poor record.

These loss-sensitive structures and Old Republic’s emphasis on customer service position the company more as a partner than a vendor, which helps explain the high retention rate. Furthermore, their customers frequently underwrite workers comp, commercial auto and general liability lines with ORI concurrently. But this partnership mentality doesn’t come at the expense of profitability.

ORI uses loss ratio as its lodestar as opposed to premium growth. This is absolutely critical to successful insurance operations - particularly Old Republic’s, in which 72% of policies are long-tail casualty lines that won’t resolve for many years or even decades. An individual working for a company in their 30s might eventually claim that the nature of their employment caused cancer that didn’t surface until they were in their 70s. In this case, the workers compensation insurer will be obligated to pay even though the employees who originally issued the associated policy are no longer with the company. This dynamic sometimes incentivizes short-sighted management to boost premium growth without care for the effect on long-term employees or shareholders. This has led to the demise of many insurers in the past and will almost certainly happen again in the future.

Counteracting this to some degree is the solid risk culture at Old Republic, cultivated over a long history of underwriting profits. The last time the company posted a specialty insurance combined ratio above 100% (indicating a loss) was in 2014 at 100.8%. Illustrating the nature of long-tail casualty lines, ORI indicated in their 2014 annual report that this underwriting loss was a result of events from 2011 and before. The company has reported a profitable combined ratio in every year since, with the ratio recently falling back towards the lower end of their 90-95% target range since 2020 after rising towards 97% in the pre-Covid period. Management measures performance over 10-year periods and consistently preaches strict underwriting standards - instructing employees to walk away from business that is underpriced. This culture, which has kept the company whole for 100 years, is essential to its continuing success.

Old Republic’s conservative underwriting and lack of significant M&A might lead some to categorize the company as staid (it does have “old” in the name). So, it’s been interesting to see the company develop a taste for organic growth in recent years. Since 2021, the company has launched a new inland marine entity (2021), an excess & surplus insurer (2022), an accident & health insurance company (2023), a lawyers specialty insurance company (2023), and a cyber insurance group (2024). Each new company is led by an industry veteran with decades of experience. This expertise combined with Old Republic’s conservative culture has led to good initial results, judging by declining combined ratios in Specialty over the past few years. These organic expansions follow a similar script for the company - diversifying into broker-distributed short-to-medium tail lines within specialty niches. This helps offset some of the long-tail casualty policies that make up the bulk of specialty insurance premiums while also offering new avenues for growth. This all seems congruent with their stated long term strategy, which seeks to drive profitable growth through organic growth, selective acquisitions, new distribution channels, new ventures, new product offerings, and geographic expansion. I’m encouraged to see that this strategy has primarily played out through organic growth as opposed to dilutive and costly M&A.

It has also contributed to heady premium growth over the past few years. Old Republic experienced 9.5% and 15.5% premium growth in 2023 and 2024, respectively, as a result of rate increases, high retention, and new business. This exhibits the company’s ability to hedge against inflation, as it has quickly pushed through commercial auto premium increases in the face of surging repair costs.

Title Insurance

Turning now to the Title Insurance segment, we find a business seemingly in decline. Premiums have nearly halved from a high of $4.4 billion in 2021 to just over $2.3 billion in 2024. This, of course, is more a result of a slow housing market as opposed to an indictment of company performance.

Almost all mortgages purchased or securitized by federal agencies (Fannie, Freddie, FHA, etc..) are required to have title insurance. This insurance protects against financial loss or legal issues arising from defects in a property’s title. This is a fairly rare occurrence, as illustrated by Old Republic Title’s loss ratio, which hovers around 2%. The company’s target combined ratio is also 90-95% in this business, meaning the majority of costs come from agent commission, title examination, and other administrative expenses.

Old Republic claims to have the third-largest title insurance company in America with 14.4% market share. As in Specialty Insurance, the company operates primarily through independent agents who control 63% of the overall market. This partnership is strengthened by ORI’s “StarsLink” product, which provides tools and resources for title insurance agents to market their business, create documents, and process transactions. Though they pay broker fees to these agents, the variable expenses inherent to this model have helped them deal with the current housing downturn, recording a combined ratio of 97 or below even as new mortgage production has faced an historic decline.

Premium volume in this business faces another threat in recent efforts to remove the title insurance requirement from select low-risk refinances. Fannie Mae is currently testing this through an 18-month pilot project. Because this initiative was launched by the Biden administration, it’s also unclear how Trump’s cabinet will interpret the results or to what extent they give more voice to industry lobbyists. Still, despite all the noise from the title industry, reducing insurance requirements on low-risk refinances does make some sense, which may eventually lead to a reduction in premium volume. I doubt any changes will happen before 2027 (the year after the pilot project ends), but I will be considering this headwind in my growth rate estimate.

Investing Float

Finally, let's take a look at Old Republic’s last source of earnings – investing float. The company’s insurance operations produce float (premium collected and held before claims are paid out), which the company invests through its $16 billion investment portfolio. This portfolio includes $12 billion in investment-grade fixed-income securities, $2.5 billion in blue-chip dividend-paying equities, and the remainder invested in short-term and “other” investments. Interest and dividend payouts from these securities generated $673 million in 2024, up from $578 million in 2023 as increasing coupons work their way into the fixed income portfolio. The average duration of fixed-income securities is 4.5 years, meaning the company’s net investment income should continue to rise as the portfolio turns over loans from the ZIRP era. I believe Old Republic managed their portfolio well through this challenging period of low-interest rates. Though the portfolio sits today at a roughly 85/15 split between fixed-income securities and equities, ORI’s portfolio managers adjusted that ratio to around 70/30 as low-interest rates resulted in depressed investment income. Starting in 2022, the company started readjusting by selling $2 billion worth of equities (all within 11% of 52-week highs) and reinvesting the proceeds back into bonds with rising interest rates. Furthermore, the company has avoided excessive unrealized losses by keeping a majority of their fixed-income investments within a short to mid-range duration. Though they did experience unrealized losses amounting to $263 million and $124 million in 2022 and 2023, respectively, this was more than offset by the $750 million unrealized gain in 2021. I expect a slow rise in investment income as premiums continue to rise and existing bonds are turned over into high-yielding securities. A $16 billion portfolio invested at a weighted average interest rate of 5% would yield $800 million, about 20% higher than the investment income recorded last year.

Balance Sheet

Given the company’s shrewdly managed investment portfolio, it’s no surprise to see them managing shareholder capital with similar care. Management behaves as an owner because most of them are, collectively holding 7.7% of shares outstanding. Old Republic has paid annual dividends for 84 consecutive years, and that payout has increased over each of the past 44. The company also signals its care for shareholder capital through relatively frequent “special dividends”, which are large payouts initiated by the board when they feel they have capital available beyond the needs of the business. The most recent special dividend in 2024 amounted to $2 per share ($500 million overall) and was issued as a result of the company “continuing to operate at a high level, generating excess capital”. Looking for other ways to soak up all that “pesky” excess capital, the company has recently developed a taste for share repurchases, spending nearly $1.8 billion to acquire 20% of outstanding shares over the past three years.

Old Republic operates with relatively low debt levels, carrying just under $1.6 billion at YE 2024. With foresight that seems rare at other companies, ORI issued $643 million of 3.85% 30-year senior notes in 2021. The company refinanced a $400 million bond more recently at 5.75% and will need to refinance a 3.875% note next year (likely at a rate in the same ballpark as their 2024 issue). But that’s it. Those three loans make up the entirety of ORI’s debt load, and resulted in an interest expense of just $77 million in 2024. This modest debt load is one reason the country’s leading insurance rating agency, AM Best, has rated Old Republic subsidiaries as “Superior” or “Excellent”. AM Best also notes that “the organization continues to have very modest exposure to asbestos litigation," which is reassuring given the long-tail risk associated with asbestos-related health claims.

Returns on Capital

So, how does Old Republic’s conservative management ultimately stack up against the competition?

Looking at price appreciation over the past decade, Old Republic looks no better than average. But change the measure to “total return” – a measure that includes dividends – and ORI’s relative position is near the top of its peer group.

ORI’s return on invested capital [ROIC] compared favorably to peers at the beginning of the past decade but has been unspectacular as of late.

This may be caused by their title insurance operations. Peer companies with large title insurance operations - First National Financial (FNF) and First American Financial (FAF) – illustrate this point vividly.

But home sales and refinancings are historically depressed and will rebound eventually. Whether that happens months or years from now is relatively meaningless to Old Republic. The title insurance segment continues to be profitable and should provide a welcome boost from today’s levels at some point in the future.

Insurance is a competitive industry, so it’s no surprise to see relatively little daylight between competition in the charts above. That said, I do think Old Republic’s historical results stand up well against competitors, even with ongoing challenges in title insurance.

Management

Craig Smiddy joined Old Republic in 2013 and, in 2019, became just the sixth CEO at the company in over a century. He took over from Aldo Zucaro, who had led the company for the previous 25 years! ORI has not taken the common step of making their CEO chairman, instead electing former general counsel Spencer Leroy to provide legal and regulatory guidance.

My personal impression of Smiddy from listening to several quarterly earnings calls is relatively positive. He comes across as unpretentious and friendly. His words seem aligned with the long-term focus championed by the company. Growth does seem to have picked up since the current CEO took over. ORI has launched five new specialty insurance operations in his relatively short tenure compared with just two in the decade leading up to Smiddy’s promotion. This has contributed to recent premium growth and falling combined ratios, though that may be attributable to a range of factors unrelated to leadership.

The recent decision to take up share repurchases has been welcome, and the record special dividend issued in 2024 shows that he values shareholder capital. It is somewhat risky to launch new insurance businesses in which the company has little experience. But I like the focus on diversifying away from casualty insurance with shorter tail lines, and the continued launch of “niche” insurance products shows that he understands the business’ value proposition rather than stretching for growth into areas in which they have no competitive advantage. Smiddy has been in the role for 6 years now, and I have few complaints. I also like how the investment committee handled ZIRP policy through 2020 and 2021, keeping risk low but wisely moving invested floats towards higher return opportunities and avoiding long-term fixed income securities.

Outlook

Old Republic has posted fantastic growth in specialty insurance over the past few years to offset dismal conditions in title insurance. Net earned premiums grew by 14% in 2024, following growth of 8% in 2023. This strength came on the back of commercial auto, general liability, and commercial property insurance, thanks to premium rate increases and the new businesses discussed earlier. This helped make up for a weak market in title insurance, workers’ compensation, financial indemnity, and home/auto warranty. While the surging growth in some of ORI’s biggest insurance categories is exciting to see, it’s important to put those numbers in perspective. Old Republic has grown at a strikingly consistent rate between 5-7% over the past three decades. Much of the recent premium growth has come from increases meant to offset inflation.

ORI has two paths through which to grow real earnings – gains in market share and margin expansion. Margin expansion is tricky in the insurance industry because any surplus profits are quickly competed away. Earning additional market share is of course possible but would be a delicate exercise while maintaining disciplined underwriting standards. The company’s recent forays into new market segments, if done prudently, should give them promising opportunities to expand. This has likely contributed to double-digit growth in their ”other coverages” specialty sub-category, though it still represents just 6% of specialty premiums overall.

Within that context, I feel comfortable projecting a 6% nominal growth rate over the next few years. That assumption keeps us within the bounds of decades of historical performance while leaving room for some upside should the company’s organic growth lead to further gains. Shareholders may also take comfort in knowing that Old Republic can lift rates quickly to offset inflation as fiscal deficits continue to run above 6% of GDP. As always, results will be contingent on management cultivating the strict underwriting standards that underlay the company’s success over the past century.

Valuation

I target a 15% return on each new investment, so this section will quantify a target price that aligns with that goal. To do this, I’ll estimate current earnings power and discount cashflows based on my growth rate estimate.

ORI earned $4.67 billion in specialty premiums in 2024 at a combined ratio of 92.2 and $2.62 billion in title premiums at a combined ratio of 97. For my estimate of 2025 earnings power, I’ve estimated growth of about 5.4% in the specialty segment, with title insurance premiums remaining flat amid a depressed housing market. Specialty insurance recorded a 2024 combined ratio towards the middle of management’s target range, but I prefer to add some conservatism for unforeseen challenges and estimate that figure at 94% going forward to account for unforeseen challenges. I also expect the combined ratio in title insurance to come down to 94% as the housing market eventually normalizes. Under those assumptions, Old Republic would earn $451 million in net earnings from premiums.

Turning now to investment income, I estimate ORI’s fixed income portfolio will generate 4.5% returns going forward while total return from equities will be 6%. This results in annual investment income of $760 million.

Finally, add in interest expense of $80 million annually after they refinance their 3.865% 2026 senior note at a rate of 5.75% and a 25% corporate tax rate and I arrive at earnings power of $850 million – good for $3.40/share.

At a growth rate of 6% for the next ten years and 3% thereafter, the discounted cash flows would be worth about $34/share assuming a discount rate of 15%. I understand that discount rate is higher than WACC, but I subscribe to the Charlie Munger line of thinking whereby discount rate should equal opportunity cost. Given my ROI goal on each new investment, this number is usually 15% for me.

Old Republic has generated annual total returns of 16.7% over the past decade and over 20% since the bottom of the quick 2020 Covid recession. The company has been a solid performer when purchased at almost any price, and a standout when purchased during times of economic worriment. Given that history and my value estimate above, I’d be happy purchasing ORI anywhere in the low $30 range. A purchase around $36 where the company trades at today should do just fine over the long-haul, but I’d prefer to wait for a market pullback to get a truly fantastic deal. A 10% drop from here would present a great opportunity for long investors to purchase a well-run company with solid growth prospects and inflation protection.

Risks

The main risk to Old Republic obviously revolves around their long-tail casualty insurance lines. The company carries about $7 billion net reserves for specialty insurance, and of the $2.6 billion in payments they distributed in 2024, about 60% were for insured events from years prior to 2023. For workers comp lines alone the company still carries $775 million in outstanding liabilities for events that occurred prior to 2015! Old Republic has successfully managed this complicated dynamic for decades, but those numbers should give you a feel for how quickly an insurance operation can be overrun by poorly written or underpriced policies from long ago.

A somewhat scary admission for most investors is that investing in this company involves a supreme amount of trust in management. Realistically, if underwriting practices change even while management continues to say all the “right” things, I’m likely to miss the initial signs of a problem. You could consider watching expense ratios like a hawk. But if you’d been following that practice back in 2014 and sold when the company recorded their last +100 combined ratio, you would have been overreacting to an abnormal year (which is common in this industry) and missed out on solid returns over the following decade.

So what is an investor to do? Avoiding this company altogether is a rational option, and one that I wouldn’t blame anyone for. Personally, I place significant importance on company culture and history. Old Republic’s history shows that it’s possible to operate responsibly and profitably in this industry over several decades. I think management has the right temperament and the company has enough historical data from many years in specialty insurance to continue successfully into the future. And though I’m not a fan of relying on credit ratings agencies to evaluate company risk, AM Best does give Old Republic stellar ratings as a result of the company’s expertise and historically solid profitability.

If historical claims data mitigate risk for Old Republic, their organic expansion into new lines then presents another peril. While the growth into select niche areas is welcome, it does bring up the possibility of writing underpriced policies due to a lack of historical claims data. The company has brought in experienced managers with multiple decades of experience to lead these new ventures, but we are again relying on management to act responsibly. There will certainly be pressure on these new hires to grow, which often leads to poor performance down the line as growth outweighs pricing discipline. One mitigating factor here is that these new insurance ventures have short tails compared with Old Republic’s traditional businesses, which should help the company root out bad policies or pricing quickly.

Summary

Old Republic celebrated their 100th birthday in 2023 – an impressive feat achieved by few companies amidst the ruthless competition inherent to capitalism. Looking through their Annual Review, the reasons for ORI’s success become plain. The company focuses on long-term results in a way I’ve rarely seen outside Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B). What other company would publish total return figures going back to 1968? ORI's compound annual return since then has been 12.8%, compared with 10.6% for the S&P 500.

Old Republic has a great culture which was built over a century of prudent management. That culture continues through today. While investors seeking index-beating returns might wait for a pullback to open or add to a position, I think today's share price represents a fair value at which to purchase a great business.

data.title: What I Wish I Knew Before Investing In Dividend Stocks
data.description:
Dividend investing has been a wonderful experience overall for me, in addition to learning a lot about the world and its major corporations, as well as learning a lot about myself, I have managed to enjoy significant total return outperformance of the S&P 500 (SPY), thanks to implementing a valuation and fundamentals-focused opportunistic capital recycling strategy. That being said, I have also learned quite a few costly lessons along the way. With that in mind, in this article I am going to share four lessons that I wish I knew before investing in dividend stocks.

Lesson #1

The first major lesson is that dividend stocks tend to be relatively poor long-term compounders, though of course there are some exceptions. The reason for this is that stocks that pay dividends, especially high yields, do so in large part because they have limited opportunities to reinvest and retain cash flows at high returns. Therefore, while they may present attractive current income and may even grow at a rate that meets or beats inflation over time, they are unlikely to be highly dynamic growers due to their limited retained capital. These are the sorts of stocks you see in a lot of high yield sectors like REITs (VNQ) such as Realty Income (O), business development companies (BIZD) such as Ares Capital (ARCC), MLPs (AMLP) like Energy Transfer (ET), and even some consumer goods companies like Altria (MO).

Of course, some of these do have very impressive long-term total return track records; however, most of their peers lack such impressive long-term compounding performance, and in the case of companies like O and ARCC, it is largely due to the fact that they have at times traded at premiums to their underlying NAVs, which enable them to issue additional shares on an accretive basis to accelerate the compounding process. Meanwhile, stocks like MO were able to grow their earnings per share at a strong clip for years with a powerful moat, but their moat is now rapidly eroding as their core smokeables business is experiencing huge volume declines and they are struggling to grow out of it.

All that to say, when you are investing in a portfolio of higher-yielding stocks like this, capital recycling needs to be a part of your strategy if you truly value long-term total return performance. The inability to reinvest a lot of capital at high rates of return makes these companies limited from a long-term compounding perspective, especially compared to dynamic growth and innovation stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Tesla (TSLA) that are constantly developing new technologies and retaining the vast majority of their cash flows to pursue growth opportunities. This is evidenced by the fact that the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ (QQQ) have significantly outperformed dividend stocks, as evidenced by comparing their performance relative to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) over the long term:

Lesson #2

Another important lesson that I wish I had learned before investing in dividend stocks is that certain types of dividend stocks can be either extremely tax inefficient or extremely tax efficient investment vehicles, depending on which accounts you hold them in. For example, REITs, BDCs, and MLPs are all pass-through entities, and so they are largely exempted from corporate income taxes, which can make them extremely tax-efficient investment vehicles.

However, BDCs primarily invest in loans, so the vast majority of their dividends are classified as ordinary income. Given that the vast majority of the total returns they generate tend to come from the dividends themselves, since they have to pay out at least 90% of their taxable income as dividends to maintain their status as BDCs, they are very tax-inefficient to hold in taxable accounts. However, these investment vehicles are excellent to hold in a retirement account, since the dividend tax classification is meaningless, while the underlying business itself is largely tax-exempt.

REITs are a bit more complicated, as some of their payouts do count as return of capital, and they also qualify for a 20% QBI deduction in many cases. So, depending on the REIT's dividend yield and the classification of its dividend, it could be a good fit for a taxable account, or in other cases where much of the dividend is classified as ordinary income and has a high yield, it is likely much better suited to holding in a retirement account.

Finally, MLPs that issue K-1 tax forms are much better suited to taxable accounts, since the distributions are largely classified as return of capital, making them very tax-efficient income instruments for a taxable account, whereas if they are held in a retirement account, they can generate UBTI, which could force you to pay a tax bill in an account that is supposed to be tax-sheltered.

Beyond that, dividend stocks, as previously mentioned, are not the best wealth compounders, so capital recycling is often a useful strategy to overlay on top of them. This is particularly true since they tend to be easier to value than high growth stocks, because you simply look at the yield for a large portion of the return and then add to that some relatively modest yet highly predictable growth rates to determine what kind of total return you are going to get from the investments. Therefore, if you do plan on implementing a capital recycling program with stocks, it is best to hold as many of them as possible in a retirement account in order to mitigate the tax headache that can come from frequent selling positions at short-term capital gains rates. Note that none of this is tax advice, rather simply my opinion based on my years of experience dealing with tax-related issues from investing in dividend stocks. Be sure to do your own diligence and speak to your own tax advisor before making any of these decisions.

Lesson #3

Another lesson I wish I had learned before investing in dividend stocks is that it is very important to understand what type of dividend stock you are investing in. For example, if it is a cyclical stock, you need to be extra careful because they can often experience sharp declines in earnings as you head into down cycles and, with it, a deep decline in the stock price. If the balance sheet is not particularly strong, this can lead to steep dividend cuts or even dividend eliminations, as I learned the hard way with Hanesbrands (HBI) and some investors who are currently in Dow (DOW) are currently learning the hard way as well.

Meanwhile, if it is a stalwart, steady stock like Enbridge (ENB), IBM (IBM), or NNN REIT (NNN), you can count on very dependable dividends, but you are not going to get much in the way of growth. As a result, these are the sorts of stocks you need to make sure you buy on a value basis and will likely want to implement a capital recycling program on to accelerate the compounding process if total returns are important to you. Meanwhile, stocks that have lower yields but high dividend growth rates are ones that can generate a lot of wealth, but you need to be careful because once the dividend growth rate slows meaningfully, the stock is likely going to pull back sharply as well. Just look at what happened to the late NextEra Energy Partners (XIFR) as an example of this.

BDCs, MLPs, and REITs generally tend to belong in the stalwart category, yet their underlying mechanics are quite different in terms of how they relate to macro factors. BDCs tend to benefit from rising short-term interest rates, whereas REITs tend to suffer when interest rates rise. MLPs, meanwhile, are much more commodity price sensitive in terms of unit price performance, even if their underlying cash flows are quite stable. Therefore, you need to make sure you realize these factors when buying these securities instead of just looking at the yield that they offer and ignoring all the other considerations that go into those investments.

Lesson #4

Finally, I wish I had known that high-yield funds are often fundamentally flawed. This is because it is very rare for a large portfolio of stocks to offer a sustainably high yield such as these high-yield funds offer. Yes, you can find perhaps one or two dozen high-yield stocks at any given time that have genuinely sustainable high yields and compelling valuations. This is what I do, in fact, in my portfolio.

However, many of these other funds are very broadly diversified, and so they generate their yields from less sustainable sources. One way they do this is by selling covered calls as the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) does, which often leads them to long-term NAV erosion risk as evidenced by the abysmal long-term performance of the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD).

Another way they can do this is by filling their portfolio with very low-quality underlying holdings, which get exposed whenever the markets experience a downturn. An example of this is the VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT), which has seen its dividend payout decline meaningfully over time.

Another option is that they may employ significant leverage risk, which can juice returns in the short term and enable them to pay out a higher yield, but also puts them at significant risk during a market crash. This is especially common in the closed-end fund space, where piling on significant amounts of leverage is quite common, as seen with funds like the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (PDI), the Cohen and Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), and the Cohen and Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF). Note that some funds can navigate carrying significant leverage quite well, but the risks are still elevated when this route is taken.

Finally, there are other funds such as the Liberty All-Star Equity Fund (USA) that pay out a high yield yet generate that yield entirely from the proceeds of selling underlying positions rather than actually generating it from dividends from underlying holdings. This exposes them to significant sequence-of-return risk and/or dividend cut risk because if the market were to ever enter a prolonged bear market, they would either significantly erode their NAV and lock in permanent losses on their underlying holdings or they would have to cut their dividend. Either of these outcomes should be a major no-go for investors looking for long-term, sustainable, and growing passive income.

Investor Takeaway

Realizing that many dividend stocks are actually pretty poor long-term compounders, that they can be potentially extremely tax-inefficient investments if you do not understand what you are doing, that they can also lead to dramatic downside risk both in terms of share price performance and dividend payouts if you do not understand what type of dividend stock you are investing in, and that high-yield funds are often yield traps due to some fundamental flaw are some very important lessons that I have learned the hard way as a dividend investor over the years. However, it is important to keep in mind that each of these challenges can be mitigated or avoided altogether while still reaping the long-term wealth and passive income compounding that comes from dividend investing. Hopefully these lessons are useful for you as you craft your own dividend portfolio, as they are invaluable to me as I build my portfolios of high-yielding, high-performing stocks at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Why I Won't Buy REIT ETFs
data.description:
I am a big proponent of REIT investing.

I write weekly articles on the sector here on Seeking Alpha and invest a significant portion of my portfolio in them.

They are today priced at near-decade-low valuations, often trading at large discounts relative to the underlying value of their properties. Historically, whenever they have been this cheap in the past, they have been very rewarding in the following years, and I don't think that this time will be any different:

Even then, I am not buying REIT ETFs such as the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) or the Schwab U.S. REIT ETF (SCHH).

They can make sense for some investors as they provide instant exposure with great diversification at a low cost and aim to provide average market returns, which have been quite respectable in the REIT sector over time.

In fact, REITs have outperformed even the S&P 500 (SPY) and Tech Stocks (QQQ) over the long run:

So why not just keep it simple, invest in an ETF, and call it a day?

Here are three reasons why I prefer to invest in individual REITs:

Reason 1: The REIT Sector is Still Inefficient

Studies show that it has become nearly impossible for active investors to outperform the broader stock market. That's because most sectors have become highly efficient, with 1,000s of professional investors looking for opportunities to earn alpha. As a result, new information gets priced in very quickly, and profiting from inefficiencies, after fees, is nearly impossible.

But REITs are an exception to this.

It is one of the few sectors of the market that remains notoriously inefficient. So much so that a recent study by Cohen & Steers found that most active REIT investors outperformed their benchmark over the past 3, 5, and 10-year periods, after fees.

The reason why active still beats passive in the REIT sector is likely because the sector is far less competitive. Most real estate investors do not trust the stock market, whereas most stock investors understand little about real estate. As a result, there are relatively fewer knowledgeable market participants following REITs, resulting in more frequent pricing inefficiencies.

Another source of mispricing is the mismatch in time horizon between stock and real estate investors. Most stock investors are highly focused on short-term results. They fixate on the next quarter, and this can cause stock prices to fluctuate significantly based on short-term results.

Real estate investors, on the other hand, are long-term oriented. They understand that real estate should be priced based on decades of future expected cash flow, and therefore, short-term results are not particularly important.

Yet, REITs will commonly sell off significantly due to short-term setbacks, providing great buying opportunities for active REIT investors who can think like real estate investors.

To give you an example:

Mall REITs like Simon Property Group (SPG) crashed very hard early into the pandemic because tenants couldn't properly operate their stores and were skipping rent payments. It led to a dip in SPG's profitability.

But any long-term oriented real estate investor would have understood that this situation was exceptional, unlikely to last forever, and the 0% interest rates would support and potentially even boost the properties' valuations.

The REIT had an A-rated balance sheet with significant liquidity, and there were no concerns of the REIT going bankrupt.

Even then, its share price crashed as if it were the end for SPG. We got to buy shares at about $50 per share, which represented less than a third of its net asset value. Just two years later, its share price had more than tripled as the market corrected this pricing inefficiency:

This is, of course, an extreme example.

But similar situations happen all the time in the REIT sector on a smaller scale, allowing more knowledgeable REIT investors to profit from the market's inefficiency.

To give you another example, we believe that Kite Realty Group (KRG) is currently mispriced, trading at the bottom of its peer group. It trades at an unusually low valuation because it is facing more tenant bad debt than its peers in 2025, which is slowing down its growth. But this sets the stage for an acceleration in growth in 2026 as it releases this space, and we expect this to result in significant upside.

Again, the market simply overreacted to short-term results because most market participants only care about the next quarter or year at most.

The inefficiency of the market is a great opportunity for active REIT investors, and ETFs do not allow me to profit from it.

Reason 2: ETFs Invest Mostly in Mega-Cap REITs

Another big issue of ETFs is that most of them are market-cap-weighted, which means that they will allocate most of their capital to mega-cap and large REITs.

These are the biggest and best-known REITs. They get a lot of coverage from analysts, and most investors tend to buy these REITs.

As a result, their valuations are typically much higher than those of their smaller and lesser-known peers. Today, large-cap REITs trade at a near 30% higher valuation multiple on average than their smaller peers:

Sometimes, the spread is justified by stronger balance sheets, better access to capital, and faster growth, but often, the premium is just excessive and largely driven by the fact ETFs and other major funds are heavily investing in larger these REITs because they get more coverage and are more liquid.

We find alpha by investing in smaller and lesser-known REITs at much lower valuations.

Reason 3: Too Spread Out

ETFs are not in the business of picking and choosing.

They invest all over the place, including the good, the bad, and the ugly.

This means that they will invest in externally managed REITs that have consistently underperformed over the long run because they suffer significant conflicts of interest.

It also means that they will invest in property sectors facing long-term headwinds.

As an example, they invest in office REITs, which are suffering from all-time high vacancy rates due to the growing shift towards remote and hybrid work. They will likely also greatly suffer from the AI labor market disruption.

They also invest in hotel REITs, which are suffering from the growing competition of Airbnb (ABNB) and booking sites. Zoom (ZM) is also reducing business travel.

They also invest in mortgage REITs (MORT), which have done very poorly over the long run, earning only 2% average annual total returns over the past 20+ years:

I think that by simply skipping these segments of the REIT market, investors can improve their returns, and this is not complicated to do.

Closing Note

For most sectors of the market, ETFs and index funds make a lot of sense.

Outperforming the market is notoriously difficult, even for professional investors.

But the REIT sector is different.

Active strategies have consistently beaten passive approaches over the long run, and our own performance is also a testament to that.

We have managed to earn 4x higher returns than our REIT benchmark since the inception of our portfolio at High Yield Landlord. The key to our success was to invest in smaller, uncovered REITs at bargain valuations and avoid troubled sectors and conflicted management teams.

data.title: 5 Stocks I'm Buying As Retail Investors Take Over The Market
data.description:
It's happening again.

I've had a growing sense that the market is entering another period of irrational exuberance, and the return of Reddit-cloistered retail traders to pump and dump meme stocks is confirming that view.

Here's where we're going today:

  • How today's meme stock mania is eerily reminiscent of 2021.
  • More on the demise of the Magnificent 7. The new king is the "Fabulous 5" AI giants.
  • Why market complacency continues to baffle me in the face of rising tariffs.
  • Some comments on my admittedly humble dividend growth buy list.
  • One data center stock on my watchlist.

Onward!

"Look At Me. I'm The Captain Now"

Retail investors are back!

One of the biggest stock market stories of this past week was about the return of the Reddit retail investors and meme stock mania (see, for example, Reuters). That extends to various crypto "assets" like Ripple (XRP), but the world of "alt-coins" is so far outside my area of interest and so deep into the absurd that I'll tread no further into the subject.

More interesting to me is the manic fervor among Redditors to collectively push up certain under-the-radar (usually fundamentally weak and heavily shorted) stocks.

Four in particular seem to have temporarily enamored the apes:

  1. Opendoor Technologies (OPEN)
  2. GoPro (GPRO)
  3. Kohl's Corp. (KSS)
  4. Krispy Kreme (DNUT)

This is just from the last three weeks. I put the S&P 500 (SPY) in there for scale. Its 2.3% gain looks like a flat line compared to the 40-318% gains of the meme stocks.

But the apes (a self-designation, mind you -- not a pejorative) do tend to get bored quickly and jump to the next meme stock.

For example, for about 5 days, they pumped up Beyond Meat (BYND), the beleaguered plant-based meat that has been struggling mightily since its brief time in the sun in 2020. But over the last few trading days, they've moved on from the stock quickly.

Easy come, easy go.

The latest meme-ified stock as of this writing is American Eagle (AEO), a clothing brand that recently announced a marketing campaign featuring actress Sydney Sweeney. Before this campaign announcement, AEO had been down 35% YTD. But it became a rapidly became a darling of the Redditors in the WallStreetBets forum pre-market Thursday.

That said, the ape fervor seems to have died down over the course of the trading day on Thursday. In fact, that's true of all the meme stocks -- except Kohl's. There's an initial burst of excitement, then some taking of gains.

Interestingly, the Barclays Equity Euphoria Indicator, which measures investor excitement via options data, has scarcely been higher since the index came into existence a decade ago:

This feels eerily reminiscent of the early stages of the retail investor mania of 2021.

Both then and now, the S&P 500 was richly valued. Both then and now, the mood of the market could be considered ebullient.

And I think it's useful to remember that one of the stated motivations of the Reddit "apes" back in 2021 was a sense of animosity toward Wall Street and perhaps also the affluent more broadly.

The rich are getting way richer, both because of the stock market and housing prices. Meanwhile, the "everyman" living paycheck to paycheck is falling further and further behind.

You can see this in the consumer sentiment surveys.

I think this mixture of extreme ebullience and extreme inequality come together to fuel the absurdist and quixotic backlash against Wall Street playing out today.

The "Mag 7" Is Dead; All Hail The "Fab 5"!

Speaking of ebullience, a different and more momentum-driven (rather than meme-driven) retail investor has been fueling a mega-rally in speculative tech stocks and anything AI-related.

For example, see the massive outperformance of Cathy Wood's ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) and Roundhill's Generative AI & Technology ETF (CHAT) year-to-date, especially over the last few months, compared to the SPY and Magnificent 7 (MAGS):

It's pretty incredible to see the once-bulletproof Mag 7 underperforming the market this year.

I think it would be a mistake to interpret this as the market "broadening out." Rather, I see it as a rotation from one momentum-driven set of stocks to another.

Compare the ~6% YTD gain of the Mag 7 composite to, for example, what you might call the "Fabulous 5":

  1. Oracle (ORCL)
  2. Nvidia (NVDA)
  3. Broadcom (AVGO)
  4. Meta Platforms (META)
  5. Microsoft (MSFT)

Keep this up and they might be able to catch up to the meme stocks!

Billionaire Mark Cuban recently asserted that "IP is KING in an AI world." This includes deep data libraries, proprietary software, network advantages, and top engineering talent.

With that in mind, it's easy to see (sadly, only in retrospect for me) how a company like ORCL would be a winner in this brave new world. ORCL's embedded advantages in cloud infrastructure, high-end data center chips, and enterprise software put it at the leading edge of the hyperscalers. And its massive existing customer base gives it the opportunity to facilitate extensive applications of AI by its customers. AI and data management go together like a hand in a glove.

We all know how NVDA benefits from an AI world -- proprietary, ultra-high-tech semiconductors are a bottleneck, and NVDA is effectively the only supplier that can serve hyperscalers that will pay just about anything to obtain its products.

Whereas NVDA is largely focused on AI and gaming applications, AVGO's competitive advantages are broader, and so is its customer base. It boasts a high-quality set of proprietary hardware that serve secondary applications of AI.

META has a massive treasure trove of data from its social media platforms, which seem to be basically impenetrable to competitors at this point. Plus, Mark Zuckerberg is dishing out hundreds of millions to poach top AI engineering talent.

Finally, MSFT's massive cloud infrastructure (Azure), embedded market leadership in software, and investment in OpenAI provide major competitive advantages in an AI world.

Many other names might come to mind as potential winners. An obvious one to me would be Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), which just reported strong Q2 2025 earnings, including 11.7% revenue growth in Search, 13.1% growth from YouTube, and almost 32% revenue growth from Google Cloud. GOOGL may not be one of the biggest players in cloud, but it's catching up. And its core Search business isn't dying -- at least not yet.

Oh, and the Big Tech conglomerate also tacked on another $10 billion to its capex budget for this year, bringing total investment (mostly AI-related) to $85 billion.

Even so, GOOGL has a long way to go to catch up to the "Fab 5" above.

The Strange Dissonance Between Market Exuberance And Tariffs

It is particularly odd to see the market so exuberant (e.g. forward P/E ratio of 22.5x for the S&P 500) at a time when economic fundamentals have been so threatened.

Now, there are plenty of positive aspects of legislative and fiscal policy today. Just extending the 2017 tax cuts is a major win and load off the shoulders of corporate America.

But at the same time, it is strange to see so little concern about the indirect tax hike on corporate America coming from tariffs.

I've been warning for a while that the current administration is not merely using tariffs as a negotiating tactic to get what they want and then take them away. Trade "deals" (to whatever extent they're representative of the final trade agreements) recently announced keep tariffs on foreign imports at 10-15% or higher. Not to mention tariffs on steel, aluminum, and various other specific items.

So here's my chain of reasoning:

  1. Tariffs on imports, whether foreign countries make deals with the US or not, are going to remain many times higher than they were at the beginning of the year.
  2. The vast majority of tariff costs are borne by the American businesses importing the goods.
  3. Instead of being offset by appreciation in the US Dollar, the effect of tariffs has been exacerbated by the weakening of the USD.
  4. Most businesses and retailers do not have the stimulus-driven tailwinds to cushion price hikes that they had in 2021-2022.
  5. Between the brow-beating of the president and weakness among paycheck-to-paycheck consumers, there seems to be little wherewithal to pass tariff costs onto consumers today.
  6. Most of the tariff costs, then, will be borne by importing businesses for the foreseeable future.
  7. Therefore, all else being equal, tariffs are likely to reduce margins and profits going forward.

The market (not to mention analyst EPS estimates) seem to be telling me I'm wrong, that there's nothing to worry about. But I can't see where exactly I've gone wrong in that chain of reasoning above.

Granted, the market is being driven primarily by AI-related and various meme stocks, as discussed above, but the Equal-Weight S&P 500 (RSP) and retailers (XRT) are up this year as well.

It's as if nobody can see the tariffs that are plainly here to stay. It's not as if there will come a moment when the president will suddenly reveal his true identity as a Ricardian free-trader.

To quote one of my favorite writers (myself) from an April 12th article:

My own view, as I've written many times, is that Trump is a thoroughgoing protectionist who believes that it would be good to substitute imports with domestic production. Trump views all outsourcing of production as theft of American wealth. It also seems clear to me that Trump views trade deficits as an indication of outsourcing and therefore US manufacturing job losses... and therefore theft of American wealth.

This is a rehash of the 400-year-old mercantilist idea that exports are good and make a nation richer, while imports are bad and make a nation poorer.

I'd argue that the recently announced trade agreements that drop foreign countries' tariffs to basically 0% while leaving US tariffs on their imports at 10-15% is an example of mercantilism.

The Wall Street Journal's Greg IP recently articulated a similar thought. The president's goal was never to achieve true reciprocity or promote free trade mutualism. Ip writes:

Trump himself never subscribed to this. He has been clear and single-minded about his goal: He wants tariffs, the higher the better. Whether that is achieved unilaterally or via deals is secondary.

Trump advisers argue that only tariffs can effectively address trade deficits, which, they say, reflect a plethora of nontariff barriers such as regulations and taxes that suppress consumption and imports. Yet since the 1980s, Trump has advanced a simpler rationale: Others should pay for access to the U.S. market or the protection of the U.S. military.

When one understands this, it becomes clear the current administration is trying to fundamentally reset the global trade system, not merely increase the freedom and mutuality of the prevailing system.

That means tariffs are here to stay.

And that, in turn, means that American businesses will pay for them.

My Dividend Growth Buy List Today

I'm agnostic about the AI-related stocks that clearly enjoy strong growth prospects but are also priced for something akin to perfection. I am naturally averse to stock valuations that only make sense assuming high rates of growth for an extended period. I'm usually ill-equipped to judge whether real-world performance will match or exceed or undershoot those assumptions.

Hence you will find that this dividend growth investor's buy list is rather more humble. Right now, it includes 5 DGI stocks and two ETFs:

Stocks Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
Artesian Resources (ARTNA) 3.7% Mid-Single-Digit

EastGroup Properties (EGP)

 3.3% High-Single-Digit
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.4% Mid-Single-Digit
Permian Resources (PR) 4.4% Mid-Single-Digit

Public Storage (PSA)

 4.1% Mid-Single-Digit

ETFs

Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX)

 4.7% Mid-Single-Digit

iShares Ultra Short Duration Bond Active ETF (ICSH)

 4.7% N/A

Water utilities like the diminutive ARTNA are utterly boring and thrive most in low interest rate environments. It will probably remain rangebound until and unless rates come down, but that's okay with me. The founding family still run the company and have rewarded shareholders, including themselves, with dividend hikes for 32 consecutive years. I like that consistency and reliability.

Industrial REIT EGP recently reported Q2 2025 earnings and confirmed what Prologis (PLD) had already revealed -- occupancy and lease renewals remain strong, but new tenant demand is soft due to delayed decision-making amid the current "cloud of uncertainty." The long-term outlook still looks very strong for industrial REITs.

Manufactured housing, RV park, and marina owner ELS also reported Q2 2025 earnings in which they reaffirmed guidance, but the market wasn't impressed. Demand from Canadians for short-term rentals declined in Q2, and management expect that to come back eventually. But I suspect the bigger force at play was a re-rating of the stock for mid-single-digit instead of high-single-digit growth. What is the stock worth if its forward growth rate is in the 5-6% area? Unfortunately, there may be more downside ahead, but I continue to like this blue-chip REIT for the long haul.

I talked about Permian Basin oil & gas producer PR last week, and nothing about the thesis has changed. PR's management team, including the young co-CEOs Will Hickey and James Walter, have done a stellar job of shareholder-friendly capital allocation since taking charge in 2022. I'm confident that strong capital allocation will continue.

Finally, PSA is one of the largest self-storage owner/operators in the nation, and among its REIT peer group it boasts the strongest balance sheet. There are significant scale benefits in the storage sector, and storage tenants (and revenue streams) tend to be quick sticky. I like the business model, and PSA is a dominant and conservatively managed player within it.

As for the ETFs, I mentioned wanting to increase my position in MLPX and its midstream energy stocks, including some involved in the booming LNG export business, and that hasn't changed.

If I have any cash left over, I'll continue building up my cash reserves in ICSH.

One Stock I'm Hovering Over Like A Hawk

As a final note, I'll mention another REIT I've got my eye on.

Iron Mountain (IRM) is a specialized data storage REIT that includes both its legacy physical document storage company as well as a fast-growing data center segment. The physical document storage side offers stable, predictable cash flows, while the data centers provide a strong source of growth.

Management claims to have visibility into a tripling of IRM's current data center operational capacity.

To be sure, it isn't cheap on an absolute basis. The stock is currently priced at around 23x operating cash flow, or 19x adjusted funds from operation (the REIT equivalent of free cash flow).

But pure-play data center REITs Digital Realty (DLR) and Equinix (EQIX) are valued at 27x AFFO and 21x AFFO, respectively.

IRM's 3.2% dividend yield (as of this writing) might make sense as an entry point assuming a high-single-digit or better growth rate, which seems plausibly achievable.

But I'll need to do more due diligence on the business as a whole, including the prospects for the physical document storage side, before making a buy-or-avoid decision.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3-Bucket System For $5,000 Monthly Income, For Early Retirees
data.description:
Introduction:

The S&P 500 (SP500) has been on a tear during the last 12–15 years. In fact, it has beaten most other investment strategies and funds during this period. To some extent, a large proportion of the gains of the S&P 500 have come from a handful of technology stocks, especially the so-called "Magnificent 7" stocks. Since the S&P 500 is a market-cap weighted index, it is currently overweighted in technology names. Will this winning streak of the S&P 500 of the past 10 years continue in the next 10 years? It is possible but highly unlikely. All that said, the S&P 500 has many positive aspects, especially for younger folks who are in their prime age and need to compound their capital for many years to come. These are the investors who should not care about the income aspect of the S&P 500, which is currently less than 1.2%. Also, they can tolerate the bumpy ride of the S&P 500 as they have a long time horizon.

However, if you are a retiree or someone who depends on a regular income stream from your investment portfolio, you can’t possibly survive on a 1.2% income yield, and you will be forced to sell shares often to raise the income. At the same time, the last thing you want during retirement is to worry about when to sell shares (or not to sell) to raise income for a living. If the market were going through some kind of correction, you may be forced to take big losses to raise income that you will probably never recover. It is easy to forget that at times, market corrections can last a couple of years (or more). One such example was after the dot-com bubble burst. Even when a correction lasts much shorter as we witnessed during the financial crisis, the stock prices may not recover to their pre-correction levels for many years to come.

In this article, we will present a balanced and highly diversified 3-bucket portfolio that is likely to provide 5% to 6% income and market-beating growth with lower volatility and less drawdowns. It will also aim to provide $5,000 on a monthly basis on a $1 million capital.

So, How Much Capital Would You need?

It depends on your goals and objectives. Do you plan to retire early? If so, you may need to plan for extra expenses in terms of funding your own medical insurance. For example, let’s say you plan to retire by the age of 55. Now, if your spouse plans to work much longer, you could obtain medical insurance at reasonable rates. But if you both, as a couple, are retiring early, then plan at least $1000-$1200 a month (or more) for medical insurance. That means keeping an additional amount of $120,000 to fund your medical insurance for the next 10 years. However, if you plan to retire at 65 when you become eligible to enroll in Medicare, you will still have Medicare premiums, but they will be much lower.

Other living expenses will also vary based on your personal situation. Is your house mortgage paid off? Will you still be supporting kids in college when you early retire? Do you live in a high-cost state? Can you downsize or move to a state where the cost of living is cheaper? So, these are some of the questions you may have to answer to be able to estimate your living expenses.

Once you know your annual living expenses, multiply them by a factor of 12 to 15 to calculate the capital you will need to retire (a factor of 15 is preferable to have some margin of safety).

Let’s work out by an example. Let’s say you are a 54-year-old and you want to retire early by next year at 55. After answering the above-stated questions, you figure out that you need $50,000 a year for your basic living expenses. Multiplying $50,000 by a factor of 15 (keeping a margin of safety), you should need $750,000 in retirement capital. Now, let’s add other expenses.

Basic amount of capital needed = $750,000.

Additional amount needed to fund medical insurance for the next 10 years = $120,000.

Add 2 years’ worth of living expenses (in a money-market account) = 2 x $60,000 = $120,000.

Total Capital needed = $750,000 + $120,000 + $120,000 = $990,000.

Let’s round off to $1 Million. This is what you require roughly to retire at the age of 55 based on your assumptions of living expenses. Sure, some folks can do it on much less, but this may be needed to maintain a comfortable living standard in most places. Sure, if you plan to do some part-time ‘gig’ during the early retirement period that results in income, this requirement of $1 million can be much lower.

Now, let’s see how well this amount of capital will be able to support you in your early retirement and subsequent full retirement journey. To calculate this, we will assume that one of the spouses would withdraw Social Security at the age of 65 (gets $2,000 a month at 65) and the second spouse would wait until 70 years of age (gets $3,000 per month at 70).

Table-1: Earnings and Withdrawals from Age 55-75, at 7.5% annual growth

Note: The living expenses are adjusted for inflation at 3% annually. The Social Security benefits are adjusted at 2.5% (for cost-of-living adjustment), but only after they start receiving them. The annualized growth rate of 7.5% (on a consistent basis) is taken, which is significantly lower than the market’s long-term returns.

Below, we will lay out an investment portfolio that should suit a large number of folks who are planning for a retirement portfolio. Obviously, not everyone will find it suitable for their individual needs or temperament. Nonetheless, we only try to provide a framework that can easily be modified or adjusted to suit specific needs.

Our portfolio will have four basic goals or objectives:

  • Moderate to High Income:

To anyone who wants to live off the income generated by their investment portfolio, they can’t do that with investments that yield 1% or 2%, unless they have a very large amount of capital saved. For most folks, who have $1 million or under, the portfolio needs to yield at least 5% but may be up to 6%. However, for folks who are not retired and have many years to go before retirement, the growth of the dividends may be more important than the current yield.

  • Growth of the capital:

Whether you plan to retire early or retire at the normal retirement age, the growth of capital is important at any stage. Inflation is never-ending and constant, and will always remain a fact of life. The long-term inflation in the U.S. has been between 2%-3% but at times much higher. So, when we talk about the total returns from any income investment, it should at least be 5% income yield plus the rate of inflation, preferably more.

  • Safety of the Capital:

The safety of your capital is important at any stage, but it certainly gains prominence after you have crossed a certain age. After retirement, it is even more critical. By safety, we also mean a lower level of volatility that the investor can tolerate without impacting their peace of mind. So, that’s why we believe that a small portion of the portfolio should utilize some kind of hedging mechanism. We will discuss this later in bucket-3. Besides, the rest of our portfolios will also be built around safer and diversified investments.

  • Maintenance and upkeep:

This is more of an individual choice. Some investors like to stay involved with their investments, while others prefer a hands-off approach as they do not have time or inclination. Folks who like a hands-off approach should focus on investment through broad-based funds rather than individual stocks.

Portfolio Structure:

We will have three components (or buckets) in this portfolio:

  • A Buy-and-Hold Bucket of Funds – 40% Allocation
  • A Buy-and-Hold DGI Stocks bucket: – 35% allocation
  • A Hedging/Rotation Bucket – 25% Allocation.

The first two buckets are of “buy-and-hold” nature and constitute 75% of the total capital. We are choosing this approach to ensure that the overall portfolio is very easy to implement and maintain going forward. These are also the “core” investments of this portfolio. Even though both buckets focus on dividends, the first one aims for higher income. The second bucket will consist of DGI stocks of companies that are large with stable sources of revenues, and also have substantial moats around their businesses. They also pay growing dividends, and we are aiming for an average of 4% plus income yield.

The third bucket (with 25% allocation) is a “Hedging” bucket. However, it is important to avoid the ongoing cost of hedging, as that would cut into total returns and add up over time. The best method for doing this without a cost is a Rotational method. That said, not everyone likes it because of the monthly trading involved. The monthly trading also takes away some shine if this is done in a taxable account. This method is best suited inside tax-deferred/sheltered accounts. Our goal for this bucket is to provide roughly 6% income and market-matching or market-exceeding growth.

(I) Bucket-1: A Bucket of Funds: 40% Allocation

This bucket will consist of a set of funds that focus on dividends and income. We will select seven funds that differ from one another in terms of the type of assets they hold, how they earn their income stream, or simply the types of asset classes. We believe 5 to 7 funds can provide enough diversification that we need. However, depending on personal preference, one can definitely go for more than seven funds. The seven funds selected by us provide an average of 7.58% income yield, assuming that they have equal allocation.

There are seven funds that we selected: (SCHD), (JEPI), (JEPQ), (AMLP), (FAGIX), (UTG), and (BIZD).

Table-2: Bucket of 7 Funds:

(II) Bucket-2: A DGI and Income Bucket of Individual Stocks: 35% Allocation

In this DGI bucket, we will select 15 individual stocks, selected on many different criteria. First, we want to cover as many different sectors or industry segments as possible. Secondly, we will try to include stocks that yield low income but are likely to provide high growth, and then some others that provide high current income but with less potential for growth.

Individual investors could definitely change or adjust based on their suitability. The idea here is to provide a template for a balanced portfolio to meet the goals of yield, growth, and safety.

These stocks/securities are: (JPM), (LYB), (BTI), (PEP), (LMT), (MPLX), (V), (MAIN), (UNH), (MAA), (UNP), (UPS), (O), (TXN), and (MSFT).

Table-3: Bucket of 15 stocks

Note: MPLX LP is a partnership and provides partnership income instead of dividends. Please be aware of the tax treatment.

(III) Bucket-3: Hedging Bucket (Rotation Bucket) – 25% Allocation

We admit, this rotational method is a bit complex and may not suit everyone. We specifically like it for income investors as it can provide a significant monthly income (usually 6%), in addition to providing hedging and market-beating growth. Three of the four securities selected provide high income (close to 8% to 10%), while the fourth one provides no income. Also, there will be times when a fund is replaced with the Treasury fund IEF (with much less income), but on average, the portfolio income will be 6% or more.

Rotation Method: The bucket is based on four securities, each being allocated 25%. Every month, we will calculate the total performance returns (including any distributions) over the previous three months and seven months for each of the four securities, as well as for the fallback security. We will assign 50% weightage to each of the two periods (3-month and 7-month performance) to calculate average performance. If the performance of any security is lower than the risk-free return provided by Cash (or 1-3 month Treasury), then it is replaced by a Treasury fund (IEF – the mid-term Treasury, in our example) for the next month; otherwise, it is retained. This process is repeated at the end of each month.

The four securities plus the fallback security are:

  1. (ADX) Adams Diversified Equity Fund
  2. (RQI) Cohen&Steers Qty Inc Realty
  3. (KYN) Kayne Anderson MLP Investment Closed Fund
