data.title: Hold The Line: Lithium Argentina Balances Efficiency And Expansion Amid Lithium Downturn
data.description:
A structural opportunity in a depressed market

In an international context characterized by depressed lithium prices and a marked slowdown in the investment of new projects, Lithium Argentina (NYSE:LAR) offers a proposal that combines operational discipline, low-cost assets, next-generation technologies, and a scalable growth platform.

Unlike other producers, the company managed not only to sustain positive margins during Q1 2025 but also advance in its roadmap towards a potential annual production of more than 200,000 tons of lithium carbonate equivalent (LCE). To this is added a more favorable local regulatory environment with the implementation of RIGI in Argentina, which substantially improves the future profitability of large-scale mining projects.

Since its separation from Lithium Americas (LAC), the company I have previously analyzed, LAR’s strategy has been clear: to consolidate its position as a producer of ultra-low-cost brine lithium, leveraging strategic alliances and advanced technology.

Its most relevant operation, Cauchari-Olaroz, located in the province of Jujuy, has already reached an annualized production level of between 30,000 and 35,000 tons, with an effective cost of $6,634/ton sold according to Q1 2025 results. The company reported revenues of $58MM in that period, with a production of 7,200 tons, and reaffirmed its goal to meet the full production guide for the year.

The 44.8% economic participation in this project represents the current core of income for LAR, but does not exhaust its potential. The joint development of Pastos Grandes, Sal de la Puna, and Pozuelos, in partnership with Ganfeng (OTCPK:GNENY), sets out a growth roadmap that could bring its total capacity to more than 200,000 tons per year, using a modular approach and direct extraction (DLE) technologies.

Operational output and financial resilience

During Q1 2025, the Cauchari-Olaroz operation executed adjustment tasks, which included scheduled interruptions for maintenance and operational advances, both with the objective of improving lithium recovery and decreasing long-term costs. Although this meant operating below potential during Q1, already in April, the plant was operating above 85% of its capacity, marking a rapid recovery.

From LAR, they are optimistic and project that the H2 will bring even more solid results, in line with this trend.

Even in a scenario marked by weak prices, operating performance was solid: the average selling price stood at $8,085/ton, with a gross spread of ~$1,450/ton, a remarkable margin compared to larger but less efficient or integrated competitors. In addition, support capex was estimated at $600-$700/ton, confirming that the operation can remain profitable even in a low price environment.

DLE technology as a strategic expansion vector

One of the most relevant aspects of LAR’s strategy is its transition to direct extraction technologies, developed with Ganfeng. The company is implementing a demonstration plant of 5,000 tons per year, approved by the province of Jujuy, which will be integrated into the existing infrastructure. This technology uses solvent extraction (SX) combined with solar evaporation to achieve higher efficiency and less use of reagents.

The objective is not only to reduce costs but also to make production more flexible in order to better adapt to market conditions and global ESG requirements. This plant will also serve as a technological base for the expansion of Cauchari-Olaroz (Stage 2), which has already submitted its application to qualify under the regime of large investments RIGI, and for the future exploitation of regional projects Pozuelos-Pastos Grandes.

Sensitivity of share value to lithium price

In order to obtain the possible value of LAR action under different scenarios of lithium prices and operational margins, I performed a sensitivity analysis. I assumed an annual production of 32,000 tons and a 44.8% stake, in line with the company’s reporting. I applied a conservative multiple of 7x EV/EBITDA and 161 million outstanding shares, and I obtained the following table:

This analysis allows us to concretely visualize the operational leverage that LAR has: even in a depressed environment, the estimated fundamental floor is $1.50 per share, while with lithium prices recovering to levels of $12,000-$14,000 and solid margins, the value could be between $3.50 and $4.36. The current market price, around $2.90, already discounts the base scenario but doesn’t include the upside of future expansion or an improved macro environment.

Valuation by DCF

To complete the previous analysis, I developed a simplified DCF model based only on the current operation at Cauchari-Olaroz. I didn't include any expansion plans in order to estimate a fundamental valuation floor under conservative assumptions.

I assumed a steady output of 32,000 tonnes per year for 10 years, with an operating margin of 40% and a lithium price of $10,000/t. The estimated attributable EBITDA was $57.3 million. The sustaining capex was $22.4MM annually ($700/t) and included a working capital requirement of 10% of EBITDA. The discount rate used was 10 percent and assumed zero terminal growth.

The result was a net present value of approximately $445 MM, implying an estimated value per share of $2.77.

The DCF supports the thesis that the market already assigns a fair value to the current operation but does not incorporate the potential of projects in the development stage, the impact of the RIGI regime, or an eventual improvement in lithium prices.

Conclusions

LAR combines three rare elements together: proven low operating cost, concrete growth pipeline, and state-of-the-art extraction technology. All this in a more stable and favourable regulatory environment than was the case until recently.

The risks, of course, are not minor: weak lithium prices, exposure to the Argentine macroeconomic context, and the need to finance growth. However, even in that scenario, the downside seems limited, while the upside potential remains.

For all this, the best option in this scenario is to hold the action and analyze the performance of the sector in the coming months.

data.title: Drill, Baby, Drill: 9 Stocks With Enough Firepower To Outperform For Decades
data.description:
Introduction

One of the worst things about investing is just how important patience is.

Making money requires a lot of patience, as explained by the Rule of 72.

Essentially, it's a quick way to estimate how long it takes for our capital to double based on different expected returns. We simply divided 72 by the expected return.

As we can see below, it takes 72 years to double one's capital with a 1% return. Based on a 10% return, that number drops to 7.2 years.

The problem is that patience isn't one of our strengths. In recent years, this has only gotten worse, as discussed by Psychology Today:

Four seconds. That's how long we'll wait for a website to load before irritation sets in. By 10 seconds, most of us have already bailed.

96%. That's how many Americans admitted to knowingly consuming extremely hot food or drinks that burn their mouths, showing a widespread willingness to risk personal discomfort due to impatience.

One minute (or less). That's how long most of us are willing to be on hold before we hang up the phone. - Psychology Today

This may explain why the average holding periods of stocks have dropped to less than one year, as we can see in the chart below, using WFE and IMF data.

Personally, I'm very patient when it comes to investing, as I usually buy stocks with a "never sell" mindset, which means, regardless of what happens on the stock market, the last thing that comes to my mind is selling investments.

However, it's not always easy, especially because I like to buy stocks in unloved sectors to improve my risk/reward.

Energy is one of these sectors, which is why I have written countless articles on this subject, including one that carries the title "My Favorite Place To Buy Stocks - And It's Not Even Close."

This is what I wrote back then:

I'm convinced my higher-for-longer inflation thesis is well on track.

It's the silent killer that's our purchasing power. And with secular factors unfolding, I don't see a sustainable path to the Fed's 2% target.

To make matters worse, tariffs and a weakening dollar are adding fuel to the inflation fire, as they set the stage for stubbornly high growth in prices and a potential second wave of inflation, although that would require an upswing in global cyclical growth, which President Trump and Bessent are working on.

That's why I'm doubling down on energy, one of the few sectors trading below historical valuations while it offers both yield and inflation-beating potential.

So, despite OPEC's unusual (to put it mildly) supply moves and cyclical headwinds, the long-term setup is compelling, supported by dwindling U.S. shale growth, tight inventories, and a dollar slump that should support commodities with a lagging effect.

The problem with these theses, and any thesis, in general, is that it takes time to unfold. Macroeconomic developments are slow, and market rotations need a while to heat up.

In 2020/2021, the same applied when investors thought value stocks were dead. It took roughly a year after the first lockdowns for inflation to rise and capital to rotate.

While 2020 and 2021 were horrible for value investors, the patient were rewarded handsomely.

Now, I expect the same to happen again.

Having said all of this, I am using this article to do at least two things:

  1. I am updating my thesis based on new findings in the U.S.
  2. I am presenting a lot more food for thought in the form of actionable ideas.

It's All About Reserves

Generally speaking, investing in basic materials and energy is all about identifying the best reserves. After all, since their entire businesses depend on adding value by extracting commodities from the ground, the biggest bottlenecks tend to be their ability to do just that.

In America, this is a huge deal, as the decline of Tier 1 reserves for oil and gas companies has caused the shale revolution to end. Currently, the mighty Permian Basin is the only basin capable of output growth, as other spots like Bakken and Eagle Ford have lost all momentum since the pandemic.

As the latest data, which was released earlier this month, shows, 2023 saw an overall decline in reserves, as proved oil reserves fell by 4%, while natural gas reserves declined by 13%.

Most oil reserves are located in Texas, with New Mexico coming in second, as the Permian Basin covers both states through the Delaware Sub-Basin.

Texas is also home to the most natural gas reserves, followed by Pennsylvania, the heart of the Marcellus Basin.

While there is no doubt that the best spots have been drilled, it's way too early to panic, as Mizuho just came out and made the case that there are at least 25 more years of oil shale wells left to drill in the United States.

According to their report, there are roughly 269,000 wells left to drill, compared to 290,000 horizontal wells that have been drilled in core basins since 2008. Back then, the shale revolution started its monumental journey.

Even at very subdued oil prices, the form estimates oil production can remain flat until 2033.

“Using the adjusted well productivity for remaining well inventory, we estimate U.S. shale can hold Lower 48 oil volumes flat for about nine years—or until 2033—below $60/bbl WTI,” he wrote.

As for U.S. shale gas, he expects remaining inventory in Appalachia, the Haynesville and other gas-weighted acreage “could sustain their share of U.S. natural gas volumes for approximately 22 years below $3.50/Mcf at the wellhead.” - Hart Energy

In other words, we may be at a sweet spot, where "we" are running out of Tier 1 resources fast enough to cause the supply side to be more favorable for higher oil prices without the risks of running so fast out of reserves that it becomes impossible to find good energy stocks.

That's what the next part of this article is about.

When Reserves & Valuation Tailwinds Meet

On top of educating us on oil and gas reserves, Mizuho also explained which companies have the best reserves, saving me a lot of work.

On the Permian basin, these four companies stand out:

  • Diamondback Energy (FANG): 25 years of reserves
  • Coterra Energy (CTRA): 23 years
  • Occidental Petroleum (OXY): 22 years
  • Chevron (CVX): 22 years

Just one of these companies has beaten the Energy ETF (XLE) over the past five years.

Needless to say, these companies don't cease to exist after 22-25 years. All this means is that their best reserves are expected to last this long without any new discoveries. Especially at higher oil prices, their profitable reserves increase. Furthermore, Chevron has a lot of non-Permian operations, which means the actual number for the entire Chevron Corporation is higher.

In fact, Chevron, being a supermajor, is almost like an oil ETF itself.

The following natural gas-focused companies were highlighted by Mizuho to have the best reserve profiles, each with at least 15 years of core reserves:

  • EOG Resources (EOG)
  • Range Resources (RRC)
  • Antero Resources (AR)
  • EQT Corp. (EQT)

All of these picks have beaten the Energy ETF XLE over the past five years.

Then there's Crescent Energy (CRGY), another inventory leader. However, it mainly operates in the Texas Eagle Ford Basin, which is why I list it separately from the others, as these were Permian oil stocks.

The reason all natural gas picks beat the XLE ETF and just one of these oil companies is that natural gas stocks were punished much more than oil during (and prior to) the pandemic.

Personally, I would give each of these stocks a Buy rating, as I am familiar with their business models. However, I want to add some more emphasis on these stocks, as each of these brings something special to the table.

Needless to say, as this is a multi-ticker article, I cannot give each pick the attention it deserves, which is why I will continue to bring up my favorite plays in future articles.

Diamondback Energy, for example, is one of America's premier drillers in the Permian. It's the result of aggressive M&A since the Great Financial Crisis, which turned a great idea into one of the most efficient drillers in all of North America.

Currently yielding around 4%, it returns 50% of its free cash flow to shareholders. As it reduces acquisition debt, that percentage will increase to 75% over the next few years. Its dividend potential is significant, with double-digit free cash flow yields even at $70 WTI.

Coterra has most of its assets in Culberson County, a major part of the Permian Basin. The company, which generates most of its income from natural gas, is benefiting from better takeaway capacity (relative pricing) and has returned 89% of its free cash flow last year to shareholders.

Currently yielding 3.8%, I think CTRA's buybacks and dividend strategy make it a great total return play in the Texas natural gas space.

Occidental has made a lot of headlines due to the involvement of Warren Buffett. I have not covered it a lot, as the company is currently trying to find its way, as it is neither a high-yield dividend payer nor a company known for exceptional performance, as it is balancing oil and gas production, midstream operations, petrochemicals, and carbon capture.

What makes this company stand out to me is the fact that it trades at less than 4x operating cash flow, a mile below the 8-10x multiples I usually apply to high-quality oil companies. Its own longer-term average is roughly 7.0x operating cash flow.

Hence, OXY is one of the companies I expect to double in the years ahead if we get some support from higher commodity prices and improved operating results.

With regard to EOG Resources, if there's one thing the company is known for, it's the ability to consistently find new places to drill, using very efficient methods that have allowed it to grow its dividend twice as fast as its average peer since 2019.

Currently yielding 3.5%, the company hasn't cut its dividend in almost 30 years and has a bigger focus on natural gas close to high-margin export facilities on the Gulf Coast.

Especially at subdued valuations, management is eager to use buybacks to improve the per-share value of the business, which I expect to pay off handsomely in the years ahead, both for its stock price and future dividend growth, as buybacks reduce total dividend payments, leaving more room for future dividend growth.

Antero Resources and Range Resources are my two favorite natural gas producers in the U.S., as both focus on ultra-high-margin operations in the Marcellus Basin. Antero has more than 20 years' worth of core reserves, while that number for Range is north of 30 years.

Even better, the overwhelming majority of these reserves are profitable below $2.00 Henry Hub, which means both companies can still drill profitably when others are forced to cut their output. This adds stability, although both have very volatile stock prices.

If I were running an energy portfolio, I would own both.

On a side note, I own Antero Midstream (AM), which owns the processing and gathering assets of Antero Resources. The strong relationship with Antero Resources and its operations was a major reason why I made the investment.

Then there's EQT.

EQT also operates in the Marcellus Basin, where it enjoys three decades of core reserves and a strong integrated portfolio of midstream assets, which have allowed it to become a free cash flow leader in its industry.

Last but not least, I have never covered Crescent Energy, which was a big mistake, as I think it's a great gem.

The company is a major producer in the Texas Eagle Ford Basin, which is why I did not put it in the same basket as the Permian players in this article. In this area, it is the third-largest producer that has more than tripled its footprint over the past two years.

Currently, it has an inventory life of at least 15 years without the need to spend any additional capital.

Moreover, because of its low valuation (the stock price influences ratios like free cash flow yield) and efficient operations, it has one of the highest free cash flow yields of the entire industry.

Currently yielding nearly 6.0%, the company aims to both grow the dividend over time and use buybacks for additional (indirect) capital returns.

If I did not run such a concentrated portfolio of just 16 stocks with already elevated energy exposure, I would likely buy a few shares at these levels.

Takeaway

Patience in investing can be brutal. However, essential, especially in unloved sectors like energy, where macro trends take time to unfold.

I would be lying if I said my "never sell" mindset is never tested when waiting for these ideas to play out. Luckily, history tends to reward those who hold and do their homework.

Right now, U.S. energy offers a sweet spot, as dwindling Tier 1 reserves tighten supply, yet companies like Diamondback, Coterra, EQT, and others boast decades of inventory, often paired with deep value and fat yields.

While the Permian and Marcellus basins are goldmines, the real edge comes from buying when others won't.

Whether it's Occidental's dirt-cheap cash flow or Antero's $2.00-proof gas reserves, the setup screams opportunity.

To me, energy isn't a quick trade. It's a slow burn.

For those willing to wait, the payoff could be explosive.

That's why I will continue to focus on this area.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk is a further decline in oil and gas prices. While this would eventually force a more favorable supply/demand balance, it would be bearish for every company I just mentioned.

Moreover, energy stocks tend to be volatile. Please be aware of this and do your own due diligence before buying any of the companies on my list.

data.title: Kroger: Post‑Albertsons Flexibility Turning Into Shareholder Alpha
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My investment thesis on Kroger (KR) boils down to the fact that the market is underestimating this company right now, despite its recent record high in stock prices. Kroger demonstrates steady growth in key indicators and is able to capitalize on changes in consumer behavior. At the moment, investors are getting the opportunity to buy a large retailer with protective properties at multiples below the sector, while Kroger itself continues to increase market share and marginality. The operating results exceed management's expectations: the company started 2025 ahead of its sales and profit plan, which has already allowed it to raise its revenue forecast for the year. The company has a high level of return on capital, comparable to what more expensive companies in the sector are doing, and so far, in my opinion, this is poorly reflected in the share price. The idea is simple: if Kroger increases its buybacks and increases the share of more marginal products, and inflationary pressures continue to subside, then all this together can become a real driver for stock growth.

No Deal With Albertsons Means Lower Leverage And More Flexibility

Kroger Co. is the largest traditional grocery chain in the United States by revenue after Walmart. Kroger's business model is classic multi-format retail: more than 2,700 supermarkets under various local brands (banners) are located within walking distance from consumers. Kroger earns primarily from the sale of groceries, fresh produce, FMCG categories, and related household goods. Additionally, significant sources of revenue are the pharmacy business (pharmaceuticals and prescription drugs), fuel (gas stations at stores), and its own production units (bakeries, dairies, etc.) for the development of its own brands. Thus, Kroger monetizes a wide basket of daily necessities: from food and household chemicals to gasoline and medicines. This is especially important against the background of current macro conditions, when even strong retailers are losing some of their traffic. In addition, Kroger's own production under CTM gives it cost control and allows it to earn more per unit of product, which becomes a competitive advantage in an environment with rising costs.

Kroger has always relied on organic growth — opening new stores, expanding its product range, and reaching new areas. But over the past few years, the approach has changed. The company is seriously investing in digital and adapting to new consumer demand. Online sales have exceeded $14 billion, which is already about 9% of total revenue, and they have added another 15% over the last quarter. Kroger develops delivery, pickup, automates logistics, and actively uses customer data. She now has information on 63 million households, and an entire Retail Media business is being built on this base — selling advertising solutions and services based on behavioral data to suppliers. Plus, let's add fintech and IT areas, which, although they still occupy a small share of revenue, are growing faster than others and add to the margin. All together, this adds up to a strategy: to maintain a strong position in traditional trading (focus on fresh, own brands, quality of service), but at the same time move towards high-margin digital and service. This transformation is changing the company's profile in the eyes of investors: now it is not only a supermarket, but also a platform that generates revenue from data, advertising, and services.

Kroger has a difficult market with competitors on every corner, from Walmart and Amazon to Aldi and H-E-B. Everyone puts pressure in their own way — some with scale, some with prices, some with delivery. At the same time, buyers began to count every penny: they look for stocks, go to discounters, switch to private brands. Inflation has slowed down, but so has revenue, as demand has become more cautious. For Kroger, this is both a challenge and an opportunity: private labels bring in more margin, and they are the ones in focus right now. Costs are pressing down — salaries, rent, logistics, everything is going up. But the Albertsons story never worked out: regulators blocked the deal, and now Kroger is going alone again. On the one hand, there will be no synergies and scale growth, on the other, we have avoided $25 billion in debt and gained freedom to dispose of capital. The money went into the buyback, and this has already been reflected in the share price.

Solid Q1 Print, Cautious Guide Leaves Upside

In the first quarter, Kroger showed weak revenue dynamics — $45.1 billion compared to last year, but if you remove the sold pharmacy subsidiary and the volatile fuel segment, everything looks better. Comparable sales without fuel increased by 3.2%, with growth mainly due to increased volumes rather than prices. In general, the company coped with the pressure — against the background of cautious consumption and falling inflation, it managed to keep the customer and increase traffic due to the price, assortment, and service.

Kroger also showed a slight increase in operating profit — $1.322 billion according to GAAP, and by FIFO metric — already $1.518 billion, with a margin of 3.4% excluding fuel. The company improved its gross margin from 22.0% to 23.0%, mainly due to reduced losses, reduced logistics costs, and the sale of special equipment. Kroger's costs went up, but it was possible to recapture some of them due to increased productivity. GAAP net income sank to $866 million, mainly due to increased interest expenses. But this did not affect the stock: EPS remained at $1.29, because the number of securities in circulation decreased by 9%. And if one-time items are not taken into account, adjusted EPS generally rose to $1.49. That is, even with increased costs and expensive debt, the company continues to increase its value per share.

In the Q1 report, the company raised revenue guidance for the full year 2025, leaving profit targets unchanged. Comparable sales are now expected to grow by 2.25–3.25% in 2025 (previously the forecast was 2.0–3.0%). This upgrade reflects a confident start to the year – in fact, Kroger has already achieved 3.2% in Q1, which is higher than the previous range, so top management has given the forecast a more optimistic tone. However, the remaining indicators for the year were confirmed at the same level. The management explained this by saying that despite the early start of the year plan, the macroeconomic environment remains uncertain. Indeed, consumer spending may slow down due to a decrease in household savings and the impact of high interest rates, while competition for buyers has increased - all of which makes the company cautious in its forecasts. Nevertheless, the CFO explicitly noted that Kroger's strategy of focusing on fresh produce, in-house brands, and e-commerce is working and ensures the sustainability of the model in the current environment.

In my opinion, such a cautious approach in forecasts leaves room for positive surprises: if the dynamics of the first quarter continue, Kroger may well exceed its profit target for the year. Personally, I expect comparable sales to grow by about 3% in the second quarter, which is closer to the upper limit of the current range. The reason is simple: Judging by the trends, both traffic and the average receipt continue to grow steadily. By margin, I predict a slight decrease in Q2 relative to Q1. (Costs are rising seasonally due to summer training, and the effect of shrink reduction could be most pronounced at the beginning of the year), however, year-on-year profit should also slightly exceed the level of Q2 2024. Thus, I think Kroger can show EPS in Q2 by a few cents above last year's $0.90 (I'm focusing on the range of $0.95-1.00), which will be helped by both sales growth and a reduction in the number of shares. If my scenario comes true, the market will receive signals that the company's annual forecast was unduly restrained. The consensus has an even more positive outlook, with $1 EPS expected.

Kroger DCF Upside And Multiple Gap Signal Undervaluation

My estimate is based on a DCF model with an unlevered FCF calculation for the period 2025-2030. I expect Kroger's revenue to grow smoothly by 2-3% per year from 2026, primarily due to the expansion of online sales, an increase in the share of its own brands, and new discoveries. In terms of operating profit, the model assumes a decrease in margin to 2.9% in 2026 and a slowdown — it will return to 3.1% by 2030, which should be helped by optimization, digital, and service segment growth programs. Capital expenditures will amount to about 2.7% of revenue, and then they will decrease to 2.3%, reflecting the completion of the investment cycle in logistics and automation. As for working capital, I expect that everything will remain relatively stable, with fluctuations in the range of 0.2% of revenue, without a serious impact on cash flows. Taken together, this approach provides a realistic FCF profile with the restoration of its stability from 2026-2027, which allows us to expect an average annual FCF yield of 2-3% on the model horizon.

I got the WACC for Kroger at 5.47% of the primarily low five-year beta at 0.56x (the remaining prerequisites are listed in the table above).

It is also worth taking a look at Kroger's assessment of the market and competitors. Kroger stocks are now noticeably lagging behind the sector in terms of the main multipliers, and the gap looks too large even taking into account the specifics of the business. According to the forward P/E, the company is trading around 15.3x, which is about 10% lower than the average retail valuation. Kroger's EV/Sales is about 0.46x, while in the sector it is about 1.5x, in other words, the market estimates Kroger's revenue to be almost three times cheaper than its competitors, although its sustainability and scale are hardly inferior. Of course, Kroger's margin is lower than that of some retailers of a wide range (for example, Costco or Target have higher gross margins), but Kroger's EV/EBIT ratio is also negative for the sector (14.5x vs 14.6x, parity). Interestingly, according to a number of multipliers, Kroger looks cheaper than its own history – for example, EV/EBITDA in the last 5 years was 7.3x, and now it is 8.4x, that is, the estimate has increased, but not dramatically. In general, the comparative analysis confirms that Kroger stock is trading at a discount to the retail sector and, in my opinion, unjustifiably. Kroger compensates for the low growth with predictable results, stable free cash flow yield, and shareholder benefits. I expect that as the company proves its ability to deliver strong earnings per share growth (due to a combination of +3-5% net income growth and 5% annual stock reduction), Kroger multipliers may expand to mid-market levels. Such a reassessment is quite possible when the external background (inflation, competition) stops putting pressure on the perception of the sector, and Kroger continues to improve its results.

Estimating the fair value of shares through the terminal value calculation in two ways — P/E and EV/EBITDA multipliers — indicates that the current market price undervalues the company. According to the P/E method, the base scenario (a multiplier of 15.0x and a discount rate of 5.5%) gives a fair price at $75, which is 4% higher than the current one, and a more positive scenario with a multiplier of 16.0x shows a growth potential of up to $78-82, that is, the upside can reach up to 12.8%. The EV/EBITDA method gives an even more confident signal: with a multiplier of 9.0x and a rate of 5.5%, the base fair price is $88, which corresponds to an upside of 22.2%, and in the scenario with 10.0x, it is already up to $104 or 44.2%. Against the background of a stable operating base, an emphasis on more marginal areas, and the recovery of comparable sales, the current price levels look quite working for entry. Kroger shares look undervalued at current levels, and I see potential for growth in them. Based on the baseline scenario, I set a buy rating with a target price of $78, which gives an upside of about +8.3% of the current quotes.

Kroger Leveraging Below Target to Accelerate Buybacks

At the end of the first quarter of 2025, Kroger had total debt of about $25.2 billion, of which net debt was $20.5 billion, or 1.69x adjusted EBITDA for 12 months. In the fourth quarter of 2024, the company deliberately raised financing to launch a major buyback instead of the cancelled merger. Despite the growth, leverage remains in a safe zone — the Net Debt/EBITDA target is in the range of 2.3–2.5x, so the current level of 1.7x leaves a margin. The debt structure is balanced: the main part is long-term liabilities, so a sharp increase in interest payments is not expected. At the same time, short-term liquidity is in order - the coverage ratio is close to unity, which is normal for retail.

Kroger consistently returns capital to shareholders. In 2024, the board of directors approved a new share buyback mandate for $7.5 billion, of which $5 billion has already been spent on accelerated buybacks, the rest is distributed through the market. Due to this, the number of shares decreased by almost 9% over the year, and after the program is fully implemented, it may decrease by another 15-20%. At the same time, the company continues to invest in development and keeps leverage under control. Combined with dividends, this gives investors a 5-6% annual return, not counting a possible upside in the share price. In fact, Kroger uses a balanced model: part of the return comes through stable payouts, the rest through a reduction in free time and an increase in earnings per share.

Risks

First of all, these are market and competitive risks: grocery retail is known for fierce competition, and the intensification of price wars or the expansion of giants such as Walmart, Aldi, or Amazon may force Kroger to reduce prices and margins to retain customers. At the same time, consumers in the United States are now saving due to high prices and interest rates; if the economy goes into recession, shoppers may switch more to discounters or reduce spending on premium categories, which will hit Kroger's comparable sales. Inflationary risk is replaced by deflation risk: after a period of rising prices, a pullback in food prices is possible (for example, lower prices for meat or milk during overproduction), and this historically leads to a drop in retail revenue and pressure on the profits of chains. I would also like to highlight the risks associated with labor relations: a significant part of Kroger's employees are in trade unions, and the company periodically faces strikes or the need to raise salaries and benefits, which may increase costs (there is a mention in the reports of a labor conflict in Colorado that affected sales). In addition, Kroger's desire to optimize its business (for example, to close 60 weak stores) carries the risk of losing market share at local levels and a negative community reaction, although the company is still managing this process competently. Finally, we must not forget about the regulatory and legal risks: although the merger with Albertsons has been cancelled, litigation between the companies is currently underway. In the worst case, Kroger may face payment of penalties or settlement of claims (Albertsons demands compensation for damages, C&S – $125 million for the cancellation of the store sales deal). At the same time, these risks are one-time in nature and, in my opinion, do not change the long-term value of Kroger. A more serious strategic risk – the impossibility of major M&A transactions due to antitrust pressure - means that Kroger will have to grow on its own.

Final Thoughts

In general, I maintain the buy recommendation for Kroger shares. Now, in my opinion, this is a rare example of a stable defensive business, which at the same time can significantly increase by increasing efficiency. Kroger is adapting to the market quite clearly — it has focused on segments with steady demand, is developing online, and at the same time continues to return impressive amounts to shareholders. According to my calculations, the possible upside outweighs the risks: the market is not yet fully pricing in future earnings per share growth and the sustainability of the operating model. Yes, the pressure from competitors remains, but even with this, Kroger looks capable of providing investors with about 8-10% of the total annual return due to an increase in the share price and payouts. Against the background of the discount to the sector and the market, this makes the current levels quite interesting. In fact, Kroger is now a clear story about a sustainable business with an emphasis on capital return and a clear strategy.

data.title: Workday: A Rare SaaS Value Buy In A Market Priced For Perfection
data.description:
Workday Inc. (NASDAQ:WDAY) is one of the few names today in the tech sector that is still trading close to its pre-pandemic levels. When most tech innovators have risen multifold on the AI hypergrowth wave and SaaS companies have also rallied strongly over the past 5 years, Workday appears to be a rare find with several supportive factors that I will latch on to. Of course, Workday has been stalling because the markets see concerns—mostly around growth deceleration and a lack of material operating margin improvements. But at current valuations, the downside at least appears limited. I believe Workday is a great value Buy with decent long-term upside potential in today's market highs.

How Valuations Have Compressed

With maturity, some valuation compression is expected. Workday now trades at a TTM price to FCF ratio of ~27.5x, which is at the lowest end of its historical valuation spectrum. Once trading around 100x in its growth days, the multiple compression is visibly factoring in concerns around Workday's market saturation and growth prospects going ahead. However, not so long ago, valuations were topping 40x even until early 2024. If execution backs a return of growth or even shows operating margin improvements, valuations could easily rerate 20-30%, with a minimum of that much upside on investments now.

What is Working for Workday

Gross margins are close to ideal. Margins have stagnated at an acceptable ~75% level for the past few quarters after improvements. There is room to grow here, too, with higher growth levels in the high gross margin subscriptions business (over consulting). Cloud infrastructure cost efficiencies over scale should also kick in over time. Any AI-led efficiency or self-service capability could reduce onboarding consulting-related low-margin revenues. Overall, the story here revolves around efficiencies and scale and optimizing the services segment—some of which is already underway and some are a natural byproduct as Workday grows. While current levels look good, gross margins have the potential to reach close to 80% over the next 2-3 years.

Apart from gross margins, what is also already working for Workday is its essential positioning—almost mission-critical. Unlike Salesforce, which forms the core of the businesses it caters to, Workday is, of course, a mission-critical but support function enabler for organizations. Today, Workday is nearly the gold standard for HR and payroll. Near-zero churn, multi-year contracts, and stickiness act almost as a moat. Its financial offerings are still nascent in terms of adoption, and offerings added from inorganic acquisitions don't seem like opening up newer, wide TAMS. I am not expecting Workday to become mission-critical in the way Salesforce is, and its positioning is already resilient. Improvements in financial adoption could be an upside.

The Concerns and Mitigants

I think the biggest drag today is the growth deceleration seen in the past few quarters. With a growth rate in the higher teens not so long ago, current growth levels of just about teens are concerning, no doubt. There are longer-term concerns around TAM saturation.

There are several ways Workday can play this. One, AI enhancements to its existing products that allow for upselling and greater pricing power. Some of it is already underway with offerings like Skills Cloud for intelligent employee skill tagging. AI integration and planning/forecasting capabilities are the primary focus now and the most realistic topline growth-supporting factor. Two offerings, such as Prism Analytics and Extend, allow for greater third-party integration and low-code custom app building using Workday as a platform. This is also well underway and shows realistic upselling opportunities.

What could revitalize TAM concerns is the three-axis growth levers in international expansion, vertical depth, and horizontal reinforcements. What I mean by vertical depth is specific solution tweaks to allow for easy adaptation in unexplored or low penetration use cases, such as schools or federal agencies. This likely requires a combination of compliance and product modifications. And Workday has taken the right steps here as well. Workday has built a FedRAMP authorization framework that allows the Workday Government Cloud to adapt to local laws and compliance and find use cases in government bodies. Workday Student is also gaining some traction, although adoption needs to pick up for it to ignite market interest. Government contracts, on the other hand, need long sales cycles. All in all, I feel Workday has the ingredients and focus right in terms of vertical depth expansion, but needs further compliance and sales fine-tuning to make things work. And yes, that could mean some ongoing costs to revitalize growth.

The horizontal expansion theme also finds similar hurdles. The Financial Management module is also there and growing, but it seems to lack the adoption rates that could re-accelerate growth. Again, a case of feature gaps and sales investments. And repeat the same for international expansion. It seems like Workday is one big capex thrust and a round of sharpening the edges around the product, away from reclaiming a new leg of expansion.

So far, that looks like some distance to cover, as management guidance points to only a marginal improvement in revenue growth for FY 2026. The subscription revenue is expected to grow at ~14% (compared to 13.4% in Q1 FY 2026) for the full financial year. I would assume the revenue growth should overall be around ~13% levels. As an investor, therefore, I will keep an eye out for big enterprise wins as an immediate trigger. More importantly, I will look for investments around expansion, as discussed before. Any progress there could open up growth, although not in the immediate term.

With a growth rate of ~13% and an FCF margin of ~27%, Workday still makes it close to the 40 benchmark as a SaaS company. Workday expects the operating margin to be around ~28% for FY 2026, which, along with their growth projections, limits downside drags below the 40 threshold (or keeps it marginally close anyway). However, the trade-off here is a lot more difficult, although there is room for operating margins to grow eventually.

Capital Allocation

I believe the path to higher operating margins goes through a trough in the immediate future if Workday has to look at long-term growth and margins. As discussed earlier, that could take up a lot more R&D and sales expenses now—something the markets may not like until headline numbers show the ROI.

R&D expenses are shrinking (as a proportion of revenue), which is great, but I think either the product market fit shows traction or R&D focus should increase. Of course, Workday could achieve both, i.e., product finishing and R&D efficiencies, particularly at scale. Which is what is happening—although R&D costs are getting better absorbed at scale, it is still showing an increasing graph. Although markets may view the current levels as elevated, I would be more than happy to see R&D expenses hold up, as reinvestment into Workday's business is the key for the next growth leg. Similarly, I am happy to see no dip in sales and marketing expenses. Workday's positioning and offerings lack the execution edge to catapult it to the next level—sales and R&D are where inputs are expected to be focused. And tangible gains in topline over the next few quarters should help justify those reinvestments.

Another major concern is the expanding stock-based compensation even today. Although buybacks have started to close the dilution gap, valuations will be pressured unless the free cash flow margin expands meaningfully. Again, I am fine with investments into the business (even SBC) because I consider Workday still in need of one more growth burst. But tangible outcomes will help the short-term cause.

Prudent and strategic acquisitions are another area where capability augmentations could happen at a quicker turnaround, unlocking further growth. Workday's most recent acquisitions, including Evisort, are more around AI/intelligent offerings and add-ons. I would like to see acquisitions that enable new TAM opportunities. Workday has historically been open to strategic acquisitions, and any significant acquisition that opens up TAM again will be extremely supportive of a rerating.

Workday is Not Broken

A significant part of my thesis sounds like there are fixes required. That is because I feel the market's skepticism around growth should be broken. Otherwise, Workday, as it stands today, still makes the mark for its valuations. It hovers close to the rule of 40. Growth is still very healthy, and the margins are still very good.

Even future growth drivers are mostly in place—Workday has done directionally all the right things to ensure continued growth. It looks like a case of one final push through a high-CapEx phase to reap those benefits. I recommend a Buy for Workday because its risks are limited even if the best-case scenarios don't unfold. On the other hand, any bounce back in growth, strategic acquisition, or even margin efficiencies could trigger a rerating very easily, leading to a 20-30% upside as a minimum. The near term (2–3 quarters) may be a game of patient investing, but investors willing to hold for a slightly longer term of 2-3 years will likely be decently rewarded.

data.title: Dow: Expected 50% Dividend Cut, Now What?
data.description:
Introduction

The current macroenvironment, although quite resilient has proven tough for certain sectors. And with implemented tariffs and uncertainty surrounding those in the future, companies like Dow Inc. (NYSE:DOW), a chemical company with more than a century of operations and a long history of paying dividends, was recently forced to cut its dividend.

And we could see suppressed sales & earnings from other businesses in the near future as tariffs are expected to be increasingly inflationary.

Dow Inc. has been implementing strategies to mitigate current headwinds and cut costs, but their 50% dividend cut seems to be a clear sign that management anticipates lingering headwinds.

As a result, I currently rate the stock a hold until I see improvements in their financials, and a clear sign of a turnaround.

In this article I discuss DOW's latest earnings, dividend cut, and where the company could be headed in the foreseeable future.

Brief Overview

As previously mentioned, Dow Inc. has over a century of operations, founded back in 1897 as "The Dow Chemical Company."

Dow Inc. has transformed over the years and is known as a chemical producer of various products across the globe, operating in 30 countries. These include magnesium and other various chemicals used in road deicing salts, fire extinguishers, and aspirin.

They currently operate in three segments: Packaging & Specialty Plastics, Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure, and Performance Materials & Coatings.

Sell-Off

At the time of writing, Dow Inc. is down a sizable 53.64% YoY as macroenvironmental challenges have hammered the share price in the past year.

Companies in the Materials Sector (XLB) have faced various challenges as peer LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) is also down double-digits. And DOW's recent Q2 earnings didn't help as the stock continued to sell-off after reporting a net loss.

The company reported negative earnings per share of $0.42, a significant decrease from the year prior that saw their bottom line amount to $0.68. This was also higher than analysts' anticipated loss of $0.17.

LYB's saw a steep drop in their top and bottom lines as well year-over-year. EPS of $0.33 declined from $1.53, while revenue of $7.67 billion declined double-digits from $9.93 billion.

Dow Inc.'s sales also declined as a result of global weakness and price declines in all regions. This was driven by lower international demand, despite slight gains in the U.S. & Canada.

Their Packaging & Specialty Plastics and Industrial Intermediates & Infrastructure segments saw sales decline from the previous quarter and year. Year-over-year sales declined 9% and 6% respectively.

Their Performance Materials & Coatings segment's performance of operating profits increasing by $152 million and sales & volume growth increasing 3% and 2% quarter-over-quarter was overshadowed by the overall disappointing performance. This was driven by lower input costs.

Deteriorating Financials

As a result, this along with expected persistent headwinds and deteriorating financials forced management to slash the dividend from $0.70 to $0.35 a share. Management stated this was to allow capital flexibility while navigating persistent headwinds.

Dow Inc. has a long history of paying dividends with the upcoming one in September marking the 456th one for the company. The dividend had been stagnant at $0.70 for some years, a sign of challenges the company has faced.

For those surprised by the cut, the company has seen its financials decline over the past few years, likely the reason for the slowdown in share repurchases. So far in 2025, management hasn't bought back any shares.

Moreover, whenever a company starts experiencing losses, that's usually a telltale sign of trouble brewing, especially for dividend-paying stocks.

So, the cut shouldn't come as a complete shock as this is likely to free up plenty of cash to assist in turnaround efforts. But with tariffs in place and more that could be coming, this could also lead to further downside and a delayed turnaround strategy.

Even when quality companies cut their dividend, it's usually hard for their share prices to recover, or see significant upside as a result over the near to medium terms.

Cash from operations declined from nearly $7.5 billion in 2022 to $2.9 billion last year. Moreover, capital expenditures increased each year over the same period, putting pressure on the dividend with free cash flow lower as a result.

In 2024, free cash flow was negative. And in their most recent quarter cash from operations were negative $470 million, down 43.5% from $832 million billion the year prior. The company also saw a $591 million restructuring and asset-related charge during the quarter.

Their balance sheet was solid despite the dividend cut with only $400 million in short-term debt and $16.2 billion in total debt. Liquidity was also solid with roughly $2.4 billion available. They also renewed their $5 billion credit facility.

Is Dow A Buy Now?

Although no investor wants to experience a dividend cut, this could be what a company needs to accelerate their turnaround strategy and navigate expected headwinds. Moreover, with a challenging macro environment currently, a dividend cut was the right move for DOW in my opinion.

But the question remains: Are they a buy now with the stock down over 50% in the past year and at $25 a share?

In 2022, DOW traded at over $70 a share. And if you believe in their turnaround efforts, then Dow Inc. could be a compelling buy at the current price.

DOW's management is working to right the ship. They plan to reduce capital expenditures by $1 billion, close multiple plants, and reduce jobs, all expected to cut costs and drive growth going forward. And while the company could successfully turn things around, upside will likely remain limited for the near to medium term.

They do offer attractive upside, but I agree with Wall Street's current hold rating. Investors should proceed with caution and watch for sequential improvements in the company's financials before considering opening a position as tariff deadlines and rising inflation could delay turnaround efforts, leading to volatility and further downside.

Investor Takeaway

Buying beaten-down, out-of-favor stocks could potentially lead to outsized gains if the company can successfully turn things around. However, the tough economic backdrop and recent dividend cut will likely result in limited upside and volatility in the near to medium term.

If looking to open a position, I suggest waiting for any sequential improvements in DOW's financials before investing. Although their potential upside is attractive and Dow Inc. could be a great long-term play, I currently rate the stock a hold.
